question:
Suppose a poll of 20 employees is taken in a large company. The purpose is to determine $x$, the number who favor unionization. Suppose that $60 \%$ of all the company's employees favor unionization.
a. Find the mean and standard deviation of $x$.
b. Use Table I in Appendix D: Tables to find the probability that $x \leq 10$. Verify this prob-ability using Minitab.
c. Use Table I to find the probability that $x>12$.
d. Use Table I to find the probability that $x=11$. Verify this probability using Minitab.
e. Graph the probability distribution of $x$ and locate the interval $\mu \pm 2 \sigma$ on the graph.
answer:
a. The number of employees polled is presumably small compared with the total number of employees in this company. Thus, we may treat $x$, the number of the 20 who favor unionization, as a binomial random variable. The value of $p$ is the fraction of the total employees who favor unionization; that is, $p=.6$. Therefore, we calculate the mean and variance:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu & =n p=20(.6)=12 \\
\sigma^2 & =n p q=20(.6)(.4)=4.8 \\
\sigma & =\sqrt{4.8}=2.19
\end{aligned}
$$

b. Looking in the $k=10$ row and the $p=.6$ column of Table I (Appendix D: Tables) for $n=20$, we find the value of .245 . Thus,

$$
P(x \leq 10)=.245
$$


This value agrees (to three decimal places) with the cumulative probability shaded at the top of the Minitab printont shown in Figure 4.11.
c. To find the probability

$$
P(x>12)=\sum_{x=13}^{20} p(x)
$$

we use the fact that for all probability distributions, $\sum_{\text {All } x} p(x)=1$. Therefore,

$$
P(x>12)=1-P(x \leq 12)=1-\sum_{x=0}^{12} p(x)
$$


Consulting Table I, we find the entry in row $k=12$, column $p=.6$ to be .584 . Thus,

$$
P(x>12)=1-.584=.416
$$

d. To find the probability that exactly 11 employees favor unionization, recall that the entries in Table I are cumulative probabilities and use the relationship

$$
\begin{gathered}
P(x=11)=[p(0)+p(1)+\cdots+p(11)]-[p(0)+p(1)+\cdots+p(10)] \\
=P(x \leq 11)-P(x \leq 10)
\end{gathered}
$$


Then

$$
P(x=11)=.404-.245=.159
$$


Again, this value agrees (to three decimal places) with the probability shaded at the bottom of the Minitab printont, Figure 4.11.
e. The probability distribution for $x$ in this example is shown in Figure 4.12. Note that

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \mu-2 \sigma=12-2(2.2)=7.6 \\
& \mu+2 \sigma=12+2(2.2)=16.4
\end{aligned}
$$
The interval $(7.6,16.4)$ is also shown in Figure 4.12. The probability that $x$ falls in this interval is $P(x=8,9,10, \ldots, 16)=P(x \leq 16)-P(x \leq 7)=.984-.021=.963$. This probability is very close to the .95 given by the Empirical Rule. Thus, we expect the number of employees in the sample of 20 who favor unionization to be between 8 and 16 .

meta_info.caption: Using the Binomial Table and Computer Software-Worker Unionization Problem
